---
title: "AI Bias & Health Equity Audit"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} · **Governance, Ethics & Equity** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Governance, Ethics & Equity track](governance-ethics.qmd){.track-back}

*Bias in AI becomes inequity in health. Learn to test for it, not assume it away.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Explain where bias enters AI systems
- Test outputs across groups, not just overall
- Decide when a biased tool shouldn't be used

## Overview

AI learns from data that carries society's inequities, so outputs can reflect and amplify them — and in health, that becomes unequal care. A landmark case: an algorithm used healthcare cost as a proxy for need, rating Black patients as healthier than equally sick White patients (Obermeyer et al., 2019).

The countermeasure is auditing: don't judge a tool on average performance; test it across the groups you serve, and be willing to not use it.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Moderate — Audit across groups</summary>
```

1. Take an AI output that varies by input (e.g., generated examples, summaries, or images).
2. Vary the **group** in the input and compare outputs for stereotype or quality gaps.
3. Document any disparity you find.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Moderate — Decide the threshold</summary>
```

1. For a real use case, write what level of disparity would make you **not** use the tool.
2. Note who should be at the table for that decision.

```{=html}
</details>
```

## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "Bias enters AI mainly through…", "options": ["Training data and proxies that differ across groups", "Random chance", "The user's mood", "The font"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Historical data and bad proxies encode inequity."}, {"q": "You should evaluate a tool by…", "options": ["Performance across groups, not just on average", "Average accuracy only", "Speed", "Popularity"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Averages hide disparities."}, {"q": "The Obermeyer (2019) case shows…", "options": ["A cost proxy under-served sicker Black patients", "AI is unbiased", "Bias is rare", "Proxies are always fine"], "answer": 0, "explain": "A flawed proxy produced inequitable results."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **These New Tools Could Make AI Vision Systems Less Biased (MIT Tech Review)** — measuring and reducing bias.
- **AI and Survey Sampling Problems (Sharon Lohr)** — representativeness and who gets left out.

## Useful resources

- [WHO: Ethics & Governance of AI for Health](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240029200) — equity in health AI.
- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool, with data rules.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Use **non-sensitive** examples to audit; document disparities and escalate decisions. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
